acuity_factor_recommendations.method_note: This output is decision support only and does not constitute a billing determination, rate authorization, or Medicaid payment decision. Actual CARE group classification and payment rates for this resident are determined by DSHS through a formal CARE assessment and rate authorization by the Office of Rates Management under WAC 388-106. Specialty contract programs — including Specialized Behavior Support (SBS), Residential Support Waiver (RSW), Enhanced Care Services (ECS), and EARC-SDC — require separate DSHS application and approval; they are not automatically triggered by the presence of dementia behaviors, exit-seeking, or any acuity factor identified here. The AFH operator, the resident's responsible party (daughter), and where appropriate the prescriber must review these recommendations prior to any placement decision.

gaps.resident_need: Secured egress / elopement-safe environment: Resident B has an active, documented elopement history — two prior elopements from memory care (one resulting in police recovery two blocks away) and two inpatient elopement attempts. Discharge plan explicitly requires an AFH with documented secured-egress capability. She was involuntarily discharged from her prior memory care facility specifically because they could not safely manage her exit-seeking.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The AFH explicitly states it does NOT provide secured-egress or locked-perimeter dementia care. There is no disclosed alternative mitigation (e.g., door alarms, wander-guard technology, specialty dementia contract). This is a direct and explicit capability gap that matches a documented, severe, and repeated resident safety need. The discharge plan requires secured egress, and the prior placement was terminated for the same unmet need.
gaps.disclosure_quote: This home does NOT independently provide: - Secured-egress / locked-perimeter dementia care
gaps.suggested_next_action: Decline admission unless the AFH can document a credible, compliant secured-egress mitigation plan (e.g., verified specialty dementia contract, certified door-alarm system with awake overnight staff protocol) reviewed and approved by the resident's care team and responsible party. If no mitigation is possible, refer to an AFH or memory care facility holding an applicable specialty contract with documented secured-egress capability.

gaps.resident_need: Overnight elopement risk with single awake caregiver: Resident B is independently ambulatory, has a documented history of nighttime elopement ('She wanders at night'), and sundowning agitation that extends into the evening. Her prior elopements occurred at night or during low-supervision windows. The AFH staffing model includes only one awake caregiver overnight.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure states 'one awake caregiver overnight' with no further detail on how a single overnight caregiver can simultaneously monitor for exit-seeking behavior, respond to sundowning agitation, and manage other residents. No supplemental overnight safety protocols (door alarms, bed sensors, wander-guard) are disclosed. Combined with the absence of secured egress, single overnight staffing materially elevates elopement risk for this specific resident.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Staffing model: two awake caregivers daytime, one awake caregiver overnight.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Request a written overnight safety protocol specific to exit-seeking residents from the AFH operator. Verify whether the overnight caregiver ratio is compliant with applicable state AFH licensing rules given this resident's elopement history. Escalate to the responsible party and care coordinator before admission.

gaps.resident_need: Management of physical resistance to care during bathing and hygiene routines: Resident B exhibits occasional physical resistance during bathing and evening hygiene. Family and operator evidence suggest a recurrent pattern beyond isolated episodes, including a strong preference for specific (family) caregivers. There is a documented source disagreement on the frequency and nature of resistance — discharge characterizes it as three isolated incidents, while family evidence implies a recurrent pattern requiring caregiver-matching strategies.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure references 'Personal hygiene assistance (showering, bathing, pericare)' but contains no language addressing behavioral resistance during care, dementia-informed care approaches, or the ability to accommodate caregiver-matching preferences. The source disagreement (S9/S10/OP8) means the true frequency of resistance may be higher than discharge documentation reflects, which could affect the level of care planning required. If resistance is recurrent and physical (as family evidence suggests), a single caregiver providing bathing assistance may face safety and quality-of-care challenges not addressed in the disclosure.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Personal hygiene assistance (showering, bathing, pericare)
gaps.suggested_next_action: Request clarification from the AFH operator on their documented approach to behavioral resistance during personal care (e.g., dementia-informed care training, person-centered care planning, de-escalation protocols). Resolve the source disagreement regarding resistance frequency with the discharging facility prior to admission. Discuss with the family whether caregiver-matching arrangements can be practically accommodated and document any agreed-upon care plan accommodations.

gaps.resident_need: Sundowning agitation management with partially effective psychotropic medication: Resident B experiences consistent late-afternoon and evening agitation (sundowning). Quetiapine 25 mg at bedtime was added six weeks ago with only 'mixed effect,' indicating behavioral symptoms are not well-controlled pharmacologically. Agitation can be difficult to redirect and includes calling out for her deceased husband.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure references 'Coordination with outpatient mental health and primary care providers' but does not specify any in-home behavioral intervention capabilities, dementia-specific behavioral management protocols, or on-call clinical support for acute agitation episodes. Given that quetiapine is only partially effective, reliance on outpatient coordination alone may be insufficient for managing acute sundowning episodes in real time. No disclosure language addresses after-hours behavioral crisis response.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Coordination with outpatient mental health and primary care providers
gaps.suggested_next_action: Confirm with the AFH operator what specific in-home behavioral management strategies and staff training are in place for sundowning agitation. Verify whether there is an on-call clinical contact (e.g., RN or ARNP) accessible for after-hours behavioral escalation. Request that the care plan include a documented sundowning protocol before or at admission.

gaps.resident_need: Advanced dementia care competency: Resident B has advanced Alzheimer's-type dementia (MMSE 13/30, oriented x1 person only), with multiple complex behavioral symptoms (exit-seeking, sundowning, resistance to care, agitation). She was previously placed in a memory care facility — a higher level of dementia-specific care — and was involuntarily discharged due to the complexity of her behavioral needs.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The AFH disclosure contains no explicit language about dementia-specific care competency, memory care training for staff, or experience managing advanced-stage Alzheimer's behavioral presentations. The disclosure is oriented toward general ADL and medication assistance. Given that a purpose-built memory care facility determined this resident's needs exceeded their capacity, the absence of any dementia-care-specific language in the AFH disclosure is a notable gap. Note: operator evidence (OP3) classifies the stage as 'advanced,' which heightens the relevance of this gap.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Request documentation of AFH staff dementia training (e.g., state-required dementia specialty training hours, person-centered care certification). Ask the operator to confirm direct experience managing advanced-stage Alzheimer's residents with active elopement and behavioral symptoms. Consider whether an AFH holding a dementia specialty contract is more appropriate for this resident's acuity.

gaps.resident_need: Family caregiver involvement preference for bathing and personal hygiene: The family has identified that Resident B tolerates bathing significantly better when assisted by her daughter or granddaughter, and this strategy has been used as a behavioral management tool. The family expects this arrangement to continue in any placement.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The AFH disclosure makes no reference to accommodating family participation in direct personal care tasks, scheduling coordination with family for ADL assistance, or person-centered care planning that incorporates family-preferred routines. While this is a quality-of-life and behavioral management preference rather than an immediate safety issue, failure to accommodate it may worsen resistance to care and agitation.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Discuss with the AFH operator and the family whether a formal care plan accommodation for family-assisted bathing (with defined schedule and backup AFH-staff protocol) is feasible and can be documented in the resident's individualized service plan before admission.

risk_register.method_note: This register is a decision-support tool produced at Stage 3 of the AFH intake process. It identifies gaps between Resident B's documented needs (drawn from discharge, family, and operator evidence snippets) and the AFH's disclosure-of-services language. It is not a final admission determination. All gaps must be reviewed by the AFH operator, the responsible party/family, and appropriate clinical and legal stakeholders before any admission decision is made. Severity ratings reflect potential risk level, not clinical diagnoses.

case.description: Fall risk only, recent fall history with FRIDs and inconsistent assistive-device use.

inputs.discharge_summary: Resident C is a 78-year-old male admitted for evaluation following an unwitnessed fall in his home bathroom that resulted in a bruised left hip and a contusion to the left forearm. No fracture on imaging; no head strike or loss of consciousness. He was discharged from the emergency department to a brief observation stay for cardiac monitoring (negative) and physical therapy assessment. Past medical history is significant for benign prostatic hyperplasia (on tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily — known to cause orthostatic hypotension), essential hypertension (amlodipine 5 mg daily), and generalized anxiety disorder (lorazepam 0.5 mg as needed, used approximately 3-4 times weekly for the past 18 months). Fall history per the resident and his daughter: three documented falls in the past six months. The first occurred in the bathroom (slipped on a bath mat), the second at the bedside attempting to stand at night without his walker, and the third while ambulating from the kitchen carrying a dish. He uses a standard four-prong walker but acknowledges that he 'forgets' to use it about half the time. Cognition on inpatient screening is intact (MoCA 27/30); oriented to person, place, time, situation; no dementia. ADLs: independent with bathing supervision, otherwise self-care without assistance. Continent. No diabetes, no skin breakdown, no behavioral concerns. PT evaluation during admission: gait unsteady without device, fair with walker; bilateral lower extremity weakness; prior PT four months ago discharged after six weeks of strengthening, but the home exercise program was not consistently followed. Disposition: declined return to living alone; daughter requested AFH placement for safety oversight, particularly overnight. Discharge plan: AFH with bed alarm, scheduled toileting, PT re-referral, medication review (consider deprescribing of lorazepam and tamsulosin).
inputs.family_notes: My father has fallen three times in the past six months — twice at night going to the bathroom, once standing up from the couch with a dish in his hand. He won't use his walker reliably even though we keep it right next to him. He insists he's fine but we worry. He is still mentally sharp and reads the paper every morning and remembers everything. He has become more anxious since my mother's stroke a year ago and started lorazepam around that time. He had physical therapy after one of the earlier falls but stopped doing the home exercises within a month. He does not have diabetes or memory problems, but his balance has gotten worse over the past year. We're hoping the AFH can keep him safe at night especially — that's when most of the falls happen. He's a private and stubborn man and may not call for help.

expected_care_plan_elements: Bed alarm and low-bed positioning
Scheduled toileting with night-time assistance
Bedside commode consideration
Walker placement and prompted-use protocol; track adherence
PT re-referral for strengthening and gait training
Deprescribing review with prescriber: lorazepam and tamsulosin (both FRIDs)
Orthostatic blood pressure monitoring (tamsulosin risk)
expected_capability_gaps: AFH must disclose overnight staffing model — most falls have occurred at night during unassisted toileting attempts; if 1:1 night supervision is not feasible, this limitation must be disclosed and mitigations (bed alarm, motion-sensor lighting, bedside commode) documented in the negotiated care plan
Resident's reluctance to call for help is a documented behavioral pattern; staff approach must compensate

care_plan.summary: Resident C is a 78-year-old male transitioning to an AFH setting primarily for fall safety and overnight supervision. He has a documented history of three falls in six months, the most recent resulting in hospitalization. He is cognitively intact, continent, and largely independent in ADLs, requiring supervision for bathing and limited assistance with locomotion. Key care priorities are fall risk mitigation (nighttime in particular), walker compliance, environmental modifications, PT re-referral, and a prescriber-led review of fall-risk-increasing medications (FRIDs), especially lorazepam and tamsulosin. Diabetes and dementia are absent; those care sections are not populated. A source disagreement exists regarding the specific circumstances of each fall and warrants follow-up.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Implement a bed alarm immediately upon move-in to detect nighttime rising without the walker.
fall_risk_care.rationale: The discharge plan explicitly recommends a bed alarm, the resident's worst-outcome fall was a nighttime bathroom event requiring hospitalization, and the family identifies nighttime as the highest-risk period. The resident is documented to skip his walker approximately half the time.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Establish a consistent, clear, and well-lit path from bed to bathroom with grab bars in the bathroom and removal or securing of any bath mats or trip hazards.
fall_risk_care.rationale: Multiple falls have occurred in or en route to the bathroom; one fall was attributed to a bath mat slip. Environmental modifications are documented as required. Nighttime bathroom trips are a recurring high-risk pattern confirmed by discharge, family, and operator sources.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Implement a scheduled nighttime toileting check (e.g., proactive prompting before resident attempts to rise independently) to reduce unassisted nighttime ambulation.
fall_risk_care.rationale: The discharge plan calls for scheduled toileting. The resident is continent and cooperative, making prompted toileting feasible. Nighttime bathroom trips are the most frequently cited fall circumstance across all sources.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Implement a walker-compliance support strategy: place walker in direct line of sight and reach from the bed, provide consistent verbal prompting from staff, and monitor compliance patterns.
fall_risk_care.rationale: The resident documents forgetting to use his walker approximately half the time despite it being nearby. Gait is documented as unsteady without the device. The family specifically notes his stubbornness and likelihood of not calling for help.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Initiate PT re-referral as part of the AFH care plan per the discharge recommendation, with structured in-home or facility-based strengthening and a monitored home exercise program.
fall_risk_care.rationale: PT was discharged approximately four months ago after a six-week program; the home exercise program was not consistently followed. Bilateral lower extremity weakness was noted. The discharge plan explicitly includes PT re-referral. The operator confirms this as a planned component of placement.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Document and monitor falls within the AFH using a standardized incident report process, tracking time of day, location, walker use at time of fall, and any prodromal symptoms (e.g., dizziness consistent with orthostatic hypotension).
fall_risk_care.rationale: The resident has an established pattern of recurrent falls across varied circumstances. Ongoing tracking will support prescriber conversations about FRIDs and PT progress, and help distinguish orthostatic versus mechanical fall mechanisms.

adl_support.recommendation: Provide supervision during all bathing activities, with grab bars in place and a non-slip bath mat secured to the floor (or a shower chair if preferred).
adl_support.rationale: The discharge summary and ADL status both document that bathing requires supervision. One of the documented falls involved a bathroom slip on a bath mat, making the bathing environment a specific safety concern.

adl_support.recommendation: Provide limited assistance or standby support for locomotion, particularly in transitional situations (rising from seated, navigating doorways, carrying objects while walking).
adl_support.rationale: ADL locomotion is documented as requiring limited assistance, and one fall occurred while the resident was ambulating while carrying a dish — a dual-task mobility situation. The resident's gait is unsteady without his walker.

adl_support.recommendation: Allow resident to remain independent for dressing, eating, personal hygiene, bed mobility, and toilet use per current ADL status, while staff monitor for any changes.
adl_support.rationale: The resident is documented as fully independent across these ADL domains. Preserving autonomy supports dignity and is consistent with the family's description of him as a private and self-sufficient individual.

adl_support.recommendation: Develop a respectful, non-confrontational communication approach for staff when prompting walker use or offering assistance, given the resident's noted privacy, independence, and stubbornness.
adl_support.rationale: The family specifically warns that the resident is private, stubborn, and unlikely to call for help. Effective ADL support will depend on staff building rapport and approaching safety prompting in a way the resident accepts.

medication_management.recommendation: Flag lorazepam 0.5 mg (used 3–4 times weekly) for prescriber review and potential deprescribing conversation, given its status as a fall-risk-increasing drug (FRID) and near-daily use pattern.
medication_management.rationale: Lorazepam is explicitly flagged in the discharge plan for potential deprescribing. The resident uses it approximately 3–4 times weekly — effectively near-daily — for anxiety since his wife's stroke. It is a benzodiazepine with well-established fall and sedation risk in older adults. The operator also highlights it as a primary medication concern and notes the underlying anxiety driver.

medication_management.recommendation: Flag tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily for prescriber review given documented risk of orthostatic hypotension, which may contribute to the nighttime fall pattern.
medication_management.rationale: Tamsulosin is identified in the discharge summary as known to cause orthostatic hypotension and is explicitly named in the discharge deprescribing discussion. Orthostatic hypotension on rising — particularly at night from a supine position — aligns directly with the resident's fall circumstances.

medication_management.recommendation: Note amlodipine 5 mg daily as an antihypertensive that may contribute to orthostatic or vasodilatory fall risk; include in the prescriber medication review conversation.
medication_management.rationale: Amlodipine is identified by the operator as a third medication raising fall risk. While not singled out in the discharge deprescribing language, it is a calcium channel blocker with vasodilatory effects relevant to orthostatic stability, particularly alongside tamsulosin.

medication_management.recommendation: Coordinate with the prescriber or pharmacist to conduct a comprehensive FRID review prior to or shortly after move-in, and document any changes or holds for AFH staff awareness.
medication_management.rationale: The discharge plan explicitly includes medication review. The resident has three documented FRIDs, all of which carry fall risk in older adults. AFH staff should be informed of any changes and monitor for adverse effects (dizziness, sedation, orthostasis).

medication_management.recommendation: Support the family and resident in understanding the anxiety management context for lorazepam use, and surface the need for a potential alternative anxiety management plan (behavioral or pharmacologic) as part of the deprescribing conversation.
medication_management.rationale: The family notes that lorazepam was initiated following the resident's wife's stroke and is linked to worsening anxiety. Simply discontinuing without an alternative plan may be distressing. The operator identifies mental health and anxiety management as a care domain.

unresolved_disagreements: SOURCE DISAGREEMENT — Fall circumstances (field: fall_risk.history_6mo.most_recent_circumstance): The discharge summary describes three falls as (1) bathroom slip on a bath mat, (2) bedside standing at night without walker, and (3) ambulating from the kitchen carrying a dish. The family notes describe falls (1) and (2) as both occurring at night going to the bathroom, and (3) as standing up from the couch with a dish. The operator synthesizes these as two nighttime incidents and one kitchen/couch event, aligning more closely with the family account for the nighttime pattern. PLAN DEFAULT: The care plan defaults to the more conservative interpretation — treating nighttime bathroom ambulation as the highest-frequency and highest-risk circumstance — pending clarification. This affects nighttime monitoring intensity, scheduled toileting priority, and the bathroom environmental modification emphasis. AFH operator should clarify fall details directly with the resident and daughter at intake to reconcile accounts and ensure accurate incident documentation going forward. Evidence: S4, S21, OP3.
open_questions_for_followup: What is the prescriber's position on lorazepam deprescribing, and is there an existing anxiety management plan or psychiatry/behavioral health contact?
Has a PT referral been placed yet, and is there a specific provider or order from the discharging physician to activate it?
What is the exact sequence and mechanism of each of the three falls? The family and discharge accounts differ — clarifying this at intake will sharpen the environmental and nighttime monitoring plan.
Does the resident have any history of orthostatic hypotension symptoms (dizziness, lightheadedness on standing) that staff should watch for, particularly with tamsulosin and amlodipine on board?
Is the resident willing to consider a shower chair or handheld showerhead to reduce standing bathing risk, given his preference for privacy and independence?
Has the family or resident identified any specific nighttime routines (e.g., what time he typically wakes for bathroom trips) that could inform the scheduling of proactive toileting checks?

Given the family note that balance has worsened over the past year, has there been any evaluation for reversible contributors (e.g., peripheral neuropathy, inner ear issues, vitamin B12 or D deficiency)?

recommendations.acuity_factor_name: Fall risk acuity factor (composite — not separately enumerated in CARE)
recommendations.wac_citation: WAC 388-106-0095 (pathological bone fracture — clinical complexity); WAC 388-106-0105 (ADL locomotion scoring)
recommendations.disclosure_support_snippet: Assistance with toileting, dressing, and mobility … One-person transfer assistance and walker support … Staffing model: two awake caregivers daytime, one awake caregiver overnight.

recommendations.wac_citation: WAC 388-106-0100 (mood indicators including depression score); WAC 388-106-0125 (Group B threshold when combined with ADL or BPS)
recommendations.disclosure_support_snippet: Coordination with outpatient mental health and primary care providers

recommendations.acuity_factor_name: Medication administration burden (operational factor; injections are a CARE clinical-complexity criterion)
recommendations.wac_citation: WAC 388-106-0095 (clinical complexity enumerates injections; oral multi-medication administration is an operational consideration, not a scored CARE factor)
recommendations.disclosure_support_snippet: Medication storage and administration of oral, topical, and eye/ear medications

recommendations.wac_citation: WAC 388-106-0105 (ADL scoring — toilet use)
recommendations.disclosure_support_snippet: Assistance with toileting, dressing, and mobility

acuity_factor_recommendations.method_note: This output is decision support only and does not constitute a payment determination, billing authorization, or rate setting. Washington AFH Medicaid payment for residents receiving CARE-assessed services is set by DSHS through the CARE assessment instrument and rate authorization by the Office of Rates Management — acuity factors identified here inform that process but do not independently trigger payment. Specialty contracts (SBS, RSW, ECS, EARC-SDC) require separate DSHS application, approval, and caregiver training; they are not auto-triggered by any factor identified here. The AFH operator, resident's family, and (where appropriate) the prescriber and DSHS assessor must review all recommendations before any care-plan or placement decision is finalized. Source disagreements exist regarding the specific circumstances of Resident C's three falls (discharge document vs. family notes vs. operator intake); this is noted in confidence ratings where applicable.

gaps.resident_need: Nighttime fall prevention: resident has a documented pattern of nighttime falls (predominantly bathroom/toileting-related), is unsteady without his walker, and forgets to use it approximately half the time. Discharge plan explicitly calls for a bed alarm.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure states only one awake caregiver overnight. It does not address bed alarms, scheduled nighttime toileting rounds, nighttime safety monitoring protocols, or proactive check-ins. A single overnight caregiver serving all residents may be insufficient to respond promptly to a bed-alarm event or to conduct scheduled toileting for a high-fall-risk resident. NOTE: There is a source disagreement (S4, S21, OP3) about whether all nighttime falls were bathroom-related, but all three sources agree that at least two of the three falls occurred at night, making nighttime risk robust across sources.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Staffing model: two awake caregivers daytime, one awake caregiver overnight.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Confirm in writing that the overnight caregiver can implement a scheduled toileting protocol and respond to a bed alarm for this resident without compromising care of other residents. If the AFH census or layout makes this infeasible, defer admission or add a dedicated overnight safety plan before admitting.

gaps.resident_need: Bed alarm installation and use: the hospital discharge plan explicitly recommends a bed alarm as a fall-prevention intervention for this resident.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure of services makes no mention of bed alarms, monitoring devices, or alert systems. It is unclear whether the AFH can procure, install, and operationalize a bed alarm, and whether the single overnight caregiver has a protocol for responding to alarm activation.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Require the AFH to confirm in the individualized service plan (ISP) that a bed alarm will be in place on day of admission and that the overnight caregiver has a documented response protocol. Amend or supplement the disclosure of services to reflect bed-alarm capability.

gaps.resident_need: Scheduled toileting program: discharge plan recommends scheduled toileting as a fall-prevention strategy given the resident's pattern of unassisted nighttime bathroom trips.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: While the disclosure mentions 'assistance with toileting,' it does not describe a scheduled or proactive toileting protocol. Reactive toileting assistance (i.e., responding when called) is materially different from a scheduled program that pre-empts unsupported ambulation at night.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Assistance with toileting, dressing, and mobility
gaps.suggested_next_action: Confirm the AFH can implement a scheduled toileting program (e.g., every 2–3 hours overnight) and document it in the ISP. Verify that overnight staffing ratio supports this without compromising other residents' safety.

gaps.resident_need: Environmental fall-prevention modifications: resident requires multiple environmental accommodations including bathroom grab bars and a clear safe path to the bathroom, given recurrent bathroom/nighttime falls and unsteady gait.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure does not address environmental modifications, grab bars, nightlights, non-slip surfaces, or bathroom safety equipment. The disclosure is silent on how the physical environment is adapted for high-fall-risk residents.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Conduct a pre-admission home safety walkthrough (or request AFH to complete a fall-risk environmental checklist) to verify grab bars in bathroom, nightlights on path to bathroom, non-slip flooring, and unobstructed walker path. Document findings and required modifications before admission date.

gaps.resident_need: Physical therapy re-referral coordination: hospital discharge plan calls for PT re-referral. Resident's prior home exercise program was not followed, and bilateral lower extremity weakness with unsteady gait persists.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure states the home provides 'coordination with outpatient mental health and primary care providers' but does not mention PT or rehabilitation service coordination. It is unclear whether the AFH will actively facilitate a PT referral or simply defer to the family/primary care provider.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Coordination with outpatient mental health and primary care providers
gaps.suggested_next_action: Confirm with the AFH operator that PT re-referral is included in the admission care plan and that the AFH will actively coordinate scheduling. If the AFH cannot facilitate this, assign responsibility to the discharging team or primary care provider and document the handoff before admission.

gaps.resident_need: Fall-risk medication (FRID) review and management: resident takes lorazepam (benzodiazepine, used 3–4x/week) and tamsulosin, both flagged as fall-risk-increasing drugs (FRIDs). Hospital discharge plan recommends medication review and potential deprescribing.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: While the disclosure covers oral medication storage and administration, it does not address FRID review, coordination of deprescribing, or any process for flagging medication-related fall risk to a prescriber. There is no mention of pharmacist consultation or structured medication review.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Medication storage and administration of oral, topical, and eye/ear medications
gaps.suggested_next_action: Prior to admission, confirm that the AFH will facilitate a medication review with the resident's primary care provider (or pharmacist) specifically addressing lorazepam and tamsulosin deprescribing per the discharge recommendation. Document the plan and timeline in the ISP.

gaps.resident_need: Walker compliance support: resident is cognitively intact but habitually forgets to use his walker approximately half the time, which has directly contributed to falls. Requires consistent caregiver prompting and reinforcement.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure mentions 'walker support' but does not specify whether this includes active prompting, cueing strategies, or consistent behavioral reinforcement for walker use compliance. Passive availability of walker support is materially different from proactive compliance monitoring.
gaps.disclosure_quote: One-person transfer assistance and walker support
gaps.suggested_next_action: Clarify with the AFH whether 'walker support' includes active prompting and compliance reinforcement (e.g., caregivers consistently cuing resident to use walker before ambulating). Document specific walker-compliance protocols in the ISP.

gaps.resident_need: Anxiety and lorazepam use management: resident uses lorazepam 0.5 mg as-needed approximately 3–4 times weekly for 18 months, suggesting underlying anxiety. Discharge-identified care category includes mental health/anxiety management.
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: While the disclosure mentions coordination with outpatient mental health providers, it does not address on-site anxiety management, PRN benzodiazepine administration protocols, or the clinical oversight required for ongoing scheduled PRN use of a controlled substance with fall-risk implications.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Coordination with outpatient mental health and primary care providers
gaps.suggested_next_action: Confirm that the AFH has a documented protocol for PRN lorazepam administration, including parameters for use and a process for communicating usage frequency to the prescribing provider. Confirm whether a delegating RN or prescriber will be involved in ongoing oversight given the frequency of use and fall-risk implications.

risk_register.method_note: This risk register is produced as clinical decision-support for the AFH intake team, operator, and relevant clinical/legal stakeholders. It identifies gaps between documented resident needs and the AFH's current disclosure-of-services language. It does NOT constitute an admission decision, a billing determination, or a clinical order. All gaps must be reviewed by the AFH operator, a qualified healthcare professional, and (where applicable) legal counsel before any admission decision is made. Source disagreements noted in the resident profile are surfaced where they affect gap severity or scope.

case.description: Diabetes + dementia: insulin-dependent type 2 with moderate Alzheimer's and resistance to injections.

inputs.discharge_summary: Resident D is a 79-year-old female admitted for management of recurrent UTI and reassessment of her diabetes regimen. Past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, insulin-dependent for the past six years (Humalog sliding scale before meals and at bedtime based on fingerstick blood-glucose values, plus glargine 30 units at bedtime; metformin 1000 mg BID), and Alzheimer's-type dementia diagnosed approximately four years ago (moderate stage; MMSE 18 on admission). Most recent HbA1c 7.8%. Two episodes of mild hypoglycemia (BG 60-70 mg/dL) in the past year, both self-resolved after juice. Additional history: hypertension (lisinopril), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin), and mild depression on sertraline 50 mg daily. Cognition on inpatient assessment: oriented to person and place but not time; able to recognize family; requires verbal cues for ADLs but is cooperative outside of insulin administration times. Behaviors: during the past 8-10 months, the resident has developed pronounced resistance to insulin injections — pulls away, becomes agitated, occasionally verbally protests; no aggression toward staff in other contexts. No exit-seeking, no wandering, no falls. Continent. Bathes with hands-on assistance; otherwise independent in eating and dressing with verbal prompting. Diet: carb-controlled, regular texture. Skin intact. Primary caregiver has been her adult daughter; daughter is now reporting significant burnout and is unable to continue four-times-daily insulin administration at home. Discharge plan: AFH placement contingent on availability of delegating registered nurse to authorize and oversee BGM-paired sliding-scale insulin administration; behavioral approach to injection times (familiar caregiver assignment, distraction technique); BGM four times daily.
inputs.family_notes: My mother has had diabetes for over 20 years and dementia for the past 4 years. I've been taking care of her at home but it's become too much for me — I have to give her insulin four times a day and she fights me on it now, even though she's gentle every other time. She knows me but doesn't always know what day it is. She is otherwise pleasant and pleasant to be around. She loves cats and old movies. She bathes with my help and is continent. She has not fallen. The hardest part has been the insulin shots — she sees the needle and gets very upset. The hospital said an AFH with a nurse who can come in for the insulin would be best. She is not aggressive at all in other situations. I'm worried about who will give her the shots and whether they can handle her resistance without making it traumatic.

expected_care_plan_elements: Delegating registered nurse identified and contracted before admission (RCW 18.79.260) for sliding-scale insulin administration
BGM four times daily with documentation; AFH staff trained by delegating RN
Injection-time behavioral protocol: familiar caregiver, distraction, calm voice; document attempts and outcomes
Hypoglycemia recognition and treatment protocol with quick-acting carbs at point of care
Resident-preferred comfort items (cats, old movies) integrated into routine
Monitor sertraline efficacy; coordinate with prescriber
expected_capability_gaps: Sliding-scale insulin requires staff to act on a BG value — this is the boundary between unlicensed assistance and nursing practice. AFH must have delegating RN with documented competency for this specific resident, not a generic nurse delegation
Resistance during a daily-multiple care task (injections) increases the risk of administration errors or skipped doses; AFH should disclose its protocol for documenting missed/refused doses

care_plan.summary: Resident D is a 79-year-old female with moderate Alzheimer's-type dementia and insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes (basal-bolus regimen) transitioning from home care with her adult daughter due to caregiver burnout. Her primary care complexity centers on a four-times-daily BGM-paired sliding-scale insulin regimen that requires a delegating RN arrangement prior to admission, combined with situational but consistent physical and verbal resistance to insulin injections. ADL support needs are moderate: bathing requires hands-on assistance; eating and dressing require verbal prompting only; she is continent and toilet-independent. No fall risk is documented. A source disagreement exists regarding the scope of her hypoglycemia history (past year vs. past 6 months) and is unaddressed by family notes. The care plan defaults to the more cautious interpretation and flags for follow-up. The daughter is the primary family contact and communication should be maintained at least weekly by phone.

diabetes_care.recommendation: Confirm and activate a delegating RN arrangement prior to admission. Insulin administration (Humalog sliding scale before meals and at bedtime; glargine 30 units at bedtime) must be authorized and overseen by the delegating RN through trained AFH staff. This is a stated condition of placement per the discharge plan.
diabetes_care.rationale: The discharge summary and operator intake both explicitly identify the delegating RN arrangement as a prerequisite for admission given the complexity of the basal-bolus sliding-scale regimen requiring four-times-daily BGM-paired dosing decisions.

diabetes_care.recommendation: Conduct blood glucose monitoring (BGM) four times daily — before each meal and at bedtime — and pair results with Humalog sliding-scale dosing per the prescribed protocol.
diabetes_care.rationale: The discharge summary and operator intake consistently confirm a four-times-daily BGM requirement tied directly to the sliding-scale insulin dosing schedule.

diabetes_care.recommendation: Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily as prescribed. Monitor for gastrointestinal tolerance and coordinate any dosing questions with the prescriber.
diabetes_care.rationale: Metformin 1000 mg BID is documented as a concurrent oral diabetes medication alongside the insulin regimen.

diabetes_care.recommendation: Maintain a carbohydrate-controlled diet at regular texture consistency. Ensure meal timing is coordinated with pre-meal BGM and Humalog dosing.
diabetes_care.rationale: The discharge summary specifies a carb-controlled, regular-texture diet. Meal-insulin timing coordination is essential for safe sliding-scale management.

diabetes_care.recommendation: Maintain a hypoglycemia response protocol. Keep fast-acting carbohydrates (e.g., juice) readily accessible at all times. Staff should be trained to recognize hypoglycemia signs and respond promptly given the resident's documented history of mild episodes (BG 60–70 mg/dL) that resolved with juice.
diabetes_care.rationale: The discharge summary documents two mild hypoglycemic episodes in the past year. Although the family notes do not corroborate this history (see unresolved disagreements), the discharge record warrants a precautionary protocol. Prior episodes were self-resolved with juice and did not require emergency intervention.

diabetes_care.recommendation: Track HbA1c trajectory; the most recent value of 7.8% should be reviewed at the next prescriber visit to confirm the current regimen remains appropriate for this resident's acuity and goals of care.
diabetes_care.rationale: An HbA1c of 7.8% is documented from the most recent lab draw. This benchmark should inform ongoing regimen discussions with the prescriber.

dementia_care.recommendation: Assign a consistent, familiar staff member for insulin administration times to reduce resistance and agitation. Incorporate distraction techniques (e.g., conversation about topics the resident enjoys, such as cats and old movies) during injection preparation and delivery.
dementia_care.rationale: The discharge summary explicitly recommends familiar caregiver assignment and distraction technique as a behavioral approach to injection-time resistance. Both discharge and family sources confirm the resident becomes agitated and physically resistant specifically when she sees the needle, but is gentle and cooperative in all other contexts. Family notes identify cats and old movies as sources of comfort.

dementia_care.recommendation: Approach all care interactions using dementia-appropriate communication: simple, calm, one-step instructions; avoid arguing about orientation to time. She is oriented to person and place but not time, and this is consistent and documented.
dementia_care.rationale: Moderate-stage Alzheimer's diagnosis is confirmed with MMSE of 18. Orientation deficits (not oriented to time) are documented by both the discharge summary and corroborated by the daughter's account.

dementia_care.recommendation: Monitor for escalation of agitation beyond injection times and document any new behavioral patterns. Currently, behavioral resistance is situational (injection-specific) with no exit-seeking, wandering, or sundowning.
dementia_care.rationale: Current behavioral profile is well-characterized: agitation and physical resistance are confined to insulin administration. No exit-seeking, sundowning, or aggression in other contexts is documented across discharge, family, and operator sources.

dementia_care.recommendation: Maintain weekly phone communication with the adult daughter as the primary family contact for care-plan decisions. Proactively update her on how insulin administration is being managed, as this was her primary source of burnout and concern.
dementia_care.rationale: The daughter is the sole identified primary contact who managed all care at home. Her burnout related to injection resistance was the direct driver of placement. Operator intake suggests weekly phone check-ins as likely appropriate given her level of involvement.

adl_support.recommendation: Provide hands-on physical assistance for bathing at each bathing session. Two-staff approach should be available if resistance or safety concerns arise.
adl_support.rationale: Bathing is documented as requiring hands-on assistance by both the discharge summary and the daughter's account. This is the highest ADL support need outside of insulin administration.

adl_support.recommendation: Provide verbal prompting for eating and dressing; do not intervene physically unless safety requires it, as the resident is capable of completing these tasks with cues only.
adl_support.rationale: Discharge documentation and operator intake confirm the resident is independent in eating and dressing with verbal prompting only, consistent with moderate-stage dementia ADL profile.

adl_support.recommendation: No routine assistance needed for toileting. Monitor for any changes to continence status over time, particularly given her dementia stage.
adl_support.rationale: Both the discharge summary and family notes confirm the resident is continent and independent with toilet use.

medication_management.recommendation: Maintain the full medication list as discharged: Humalog sliding scale (before meals and bedtime), glargine 30 units (bedtime), metformin 1000 mg BID, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and sertraline 50 mg daily. All medications require accurate transcription to the AFH medication administration record (MAR) and prescriber verification at intake.
medication_management.rationale: The complete medication list is documented in the discharge summary covering diabetes management, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and depression.

medication_management.recommendation: Sertraline 50 mg daily should be administered consistently and its behavioral effects monitored. Given the resident's diagnosis of mild depression alongside moderate Alzheimer's dementia, changes in mood or behavior should be communicated to the prescriber.
medication_management.rationale: Sertraline 50 mg daily is prescribed for documented mild depression as part of the resident's discharge medication regimen.

medication_management.recommendation: Insulin doses (Humalog sliding scale and glargine) must be administered only by or under the active oversight of the delegating RN. Staff may not independently adjust dosing. The sliding-scale Humalog dose is directly paired to each BGM reading and requires four documented assessments daily.
medication_management.rationale: Both discharge and operator sources confirm the delegating RN arrangement is a condition of placement, and the sliding-scale protocol requires BGM-linked dosing decisions four times daily.

unresolved_disagreements: HYPOGLYCEMIA HISTORY SCOPE — The discharge summary documents two mild hypoglycemic episodes (BG 60–70 mg/dL) 'in the past year,' which is broader than the 6-month window typically used for AFH acuity review. The family notes make no mention of any hypoglycemic episodes at all, neither confirming nor denying the discharge claim. It is unclear whether the family was unaware of these episodes, whether they occurred during a hospitalization the family did not witness, or whether the discharge documentation is imprecise. PLAN DEFAULT: The care plan defaults to the more cautious interpretation and treats hypoglycemia risk as a present and documented concern, maintaining a standing hypoglycemia response protocol. FOLLOW-UP NEEDED: Clarify with the prescriber or discharge team whether the two documented episodes occurred within the past 6 months, and verify whether the daughter is aware of these events.
open_questions_for_followup: 1. Has the delegating RN been identified and confirmed as available prior to admission? This is a stated condition of placement per the discharge plan and must be resolved before move-in.
2. What is the daughter's full name and contact phone number? No identifying contact details were provided in family notes or operator intake.

3. Can the prescriber clarify whether the two documented hypoglycemic episodes occurred within the past 6 months specifically, and whether the current sliding-scale protocol should include any adjusted low-BG thresholds or modified response steps given the resident's dementia (reduced ability to self-report symptoms)?
4. Has the current sertraline regimen been reviewed recently for efficacy and appropriateness given the progression of Alzheimer's dementia to moderate stage?

5. Are there any additional behavioral strategies the discharge team or prescriber recommends for injection-time resistance beyond familiar staff assignment and distraction — e.g., topical anesthetic, timing adjustments, or prescriber-guided behavioral consultation?
6. Does the resident have an advance directive, POLST, or durable power of attorney for healthcare on file? Not referenced in any source documents.
7. What is the renal function status (eGFR)? Metformin 1000 mg BID is prescribed, and monitoring of renal function is relevant for ongoing safety at this age and with this medication.

acuity_factor_recommendations.method_note: This output is decision support only and does not constitute a DSHS CARE assessment, rate authorization, or payment determination. Acuity factor recommendations are based on intake documents (hospital discharge summary, family notes, and AFH operator disclosure) and are subject to verification by the AFH operator, family, and relevant clinical staff. Final CARE scoring and Medicaid rate authorization are performed exclusively by DSHS through the formal CARE assessment process and rate authorization by the Office of Rates Management. Specialty contracts (SBS, RSW, ECS, EARC-SDC) require separate DSHS application and approval and are NOT automatically triggered by the presence of behavioral symptoms, dementia diagnosis, or any factor identified here. Source disagreements noted in the profile (particularly regarding hypoglycemia history scope) have been factored into confidence ratings where applicable.

gaps.resident_need: Basal-bolus insulin administration (Humalog sliding-scale 4x/day + glargine 30 units nightly) requiring a delegating RN arrangement prior to admission
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure names insulin administration as a service the home does NOT independently provide, conditional on a delegating RN arrangement prior to admission. The discharge documentation makes the same requirement explicit. However, neither the disclosure nor any other document provided confirms that a delegating RN has actually been identified, contracted, or confirmed as available. The precondition ('arrangement via delegating RN required prior to admission') is stated but its fulfillment is unverified. Without confirmed RN delegation, the home cannot lawfully administer insulin, creating a high safety risk for a resident on a complex 4x/day sliding-scale regimen.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Sliding-scale or basal-bolus insulin administration (arrangement via delegating RN required prior to admission)
gaps.suggested_next_action: Before admission, obtain written confirmation that a delegating RN has been contracted and is available to authorize, train, and supervise AFH staff for this specific resident's insulin regimen. Document the RN's license number, delegation agreement, and on-call availability. Do not admit until this is in place.

gaps.resident_need: Behavioral management protocol for consistent, predictable physical and verbal resistance to insulin injections in a resident with moderate Alzheimer's dementia — requiring a dementia-informed, injection-specific de-escalation approach
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: Multiple sources document that the resident reliably becomes agitated, pulls away physically, and verbally protests specifically during insulin injections — the exact task that must occur 4x/day. The disclosure lists general personal hygiene assistance and coordination with outpatient mental health, but contains no language addressing dementia-specific behavioral intervention during clinical tasks such as injections, no mention of staff training in trauma-informed or dementia-care approaches for care resistance, and no protocol for managing injection refusal. This gap is particularly high-risk because resistance to insulin administration is not incidental — it directly intersects with the most medically complex element of her care.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Coordination with outpatient mental health and primary care providers
gaps.suggested_next_action: Prior to admission, confirm that at least the daytime care staff have documented dementia-care and behavioral de-escalation training. Develop a resident-specific injection protocol in collaboration with the delegating RN that addresses behavioral resistance (e.g., preferred sequence, comfort objects, staff approach). Include this protocol in the individualized care plan and review it at the care conference with the daughter.

gaps.resident_need: Hypoglycemia recognition, response, and on-call clinical support — resident has documented history of hypoglycemic episodes (BG 60–70 mg/dL) occurring in the context of a 4x/day sliding-scale insulin regimen
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The discharge record documents two mild hypoglycemic episodes within the past year (self-resolved with juice). There is a source disagreement: family notes make no mention of hypoglycemia, and the discharge scopes episodes to 'the past year' rather than explicitly within 6 months (see source_disagreements field, snippets S11 and OP8). Regardless of this disagreement, the sliding-scale insulin regimen itself creates ongoing hypoglycemia risk. The disclosure is entirely silent on hypoglycemia recognition procedures, staff competency to respond to hypoglycemia, availability of rescue carbohydrates, when to call 911, or how to escalate to the delegating RN if a hypoglycemic event occurs overnight when only one caregiver is on duty. The overnight single-caregiver staffing model compounds this risk.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Require that the delegating RN develop and document a hypoglycemia action plan specific to this resident (target BG range, juice/glucose tablet protocol, threshold for 911, on-call RN contact). Confirm all staff — including the single overnight caregiver — are trained to recognize and respond to hypoglycemia. Add hypoglycemia response to the individualized care plan before admission. Note: the source disagreement regarding the frequency/recency of past episodes (S11 vs. OP8) should be resolved with the discharging facility before admission.

gaps.resident_need: Overnight insulin administration and BGM — the sliding-scale regimen requires a bedtime fingerstick and corresponding Humalog dose, occurring during the overnight staffing window when only one caregiver is on duty
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The resident's regimen requires BGM and a sliding-scale insulin dose at bedtime, which falls within — or at the boundary of — the overnight staffing period when the disclosed model provides only one awake caregiver. The disclosure does not address whether a single overnight caregiver is trained and delegated for insulin administration, or how simultaneous resident needs (e.g., another resident requiring assistance) would be managed while the caregiver is occupied with a multi-step insulin administration process for a resident who is physically resistant.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Staffing model: two awake caregivers daytime, one awake caregiver overnight.
gaps.suggested_next_action: Clarify with the AFH operator and delegating RN whether the single overnight caregiver is (or will be) included in the delegation agreement and trained for this resident's insulin protocol. Confirm the home's capacity to safely administer bedtime insulin given potential competing resident care demands overnight. If the overnight caregiver cannot be delegated or the staffing ratio creates safety concerns, this should be addressed as a condition of admission.

gaps.resident_need: Carbohydrate-controlled diet — resident requires consistent carb-controlled meals to support glycemic management in the context of a sliding-scale insulin regimen
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure explicitly lists 'Carb-controlled and renal-modified diets' as a provided service, and the resident's diet need is carb-controlled, regular texture. This appears well-matched. However, the disclosure does not specify how carb content is tracked or communicated to staff administering sliding-scale insulin. Given that sliding-scale dosing is keyed to pre-meal BGM readings — which are influenced by prior meal carb content — there is a weak integration gap between dietary management and insulin administration protocols. This is a lower-severity operational risk, not a fundamental service gap.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Carb-controlled and renal-modified diets
gaps.suggested_next_action: Confirm with the AFH operator that meal carb content is consistently documented and communicated to the staff member performing pre-meal BGM and sliding-scale dosing. Include dietary-insulin coordination in the individualized care plan reviewed with the delegating RN.

gaps.resident_need: Dementia-informed care planning for moderate-stage Alzheimer's — resident requires consistent, person-centered approaches for ADL assistance (bathing with hands-on help, verbal prompting for eating/dressing) and behavioral support
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure lists personal hygiene assistance and assistance with dressing/toileting, which aligns with the resident's ADL needs. However, the disclosure contains no language specifying staff training in dementia care, person-centered approaches, or management of cognitive impairment during ADLs. For a resident with confirmed moderate Alzheimer's, the absence of any dementia-care competency language in the disclosure is a gap — particularly given documented situational agitation and physical resistance. This is a medium-severity gap because the ADL needs themselves (hands-on bathing, verbal prompting) are within typical AFH scope, but the behavioral and cognitive overlay is not addressed.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Personal hygiene assistance (showering, bathing, pericare)
gaps.suggested_next_action: Request documentation of staff dementia-care training (e.g., Washington State DSHS-recognized dementia training hours or equivalent). Ensure the individualized care plan includes dementia-specific guidance for bathing and ADL assistance. Involve the daughter in care-plan development given her knowledge of the resident's preferences and triggers.

gaps.resident_need: Psychiatric medication management — resident is on sertraline 50 mg daily for mild depression, requiring consistent oral medication administration and monitoring for behavioral or mood changes in the context of moderate dementia
gaps.missing_or_weak_support: The disclosure covers 'oral medications' under medication storage and administration, which nominally covers sertraline. However, the disclosure does not address monitoring for sertraline-related behavioral side effects (e.g., increased agitation, serotonin syndrome signs) in a cognitively impaired resident, nor does it specify how behavioral/mood changes would be escalated to the prescriber. This is a low-severity gap given the medication is oral and the dose is modest, but worth noting in the care plan.
gaps.disclosure_quote: Medication storage and administration of oral, topical, and eye/ear medications
gaps.suggested_next_action: Include sertraline in the individualized medication administration record with a notation to monitor for behavioral changes. Confirm the home's process for escalating mood or behavioral concerns to the primary care provider. Coordinate with outpatient mental health per the disclosure's stated coordination capability.

risk_register.method_note: This register is produced as Stage 3 decision-support for AFH intake review. It identifies capability gaps between Resident D's documented clinical and behavioral needs and the AFH's disclosure-of-services language. It does not constitute a clinical determination, legal opinion, or admission decision. All gaps must be reviewed by the AFH operator, the delegating RN (where applicable), the resident's primary care provider, the family/designated decision-maker, and any relevant licensing or oversight authorities before an admission decision is finalized.

case.description: Dementia + fall risk: advanced Alzheimer's with recent falls, FRID polypharmacy, and cognitive-mobility mismatch.

inputs.discharge_summary: Resident E is an 84-year-old female admitted from her prior memory-care facility following an unwitnessed bedside fall at night with a head bump and small forehead laceration (no concussion on imaging; sutures placed and removed prior to discharge). Past medical history is significant for advanced-stage Alzheimer's-type dementia (current cognitive assessment: fully disoriented to person, place, and time; MMSE not completable beyond 4/30 on admission), hypertension, and chronic insomnia. Current medications: donepezil 10 mg daily, memantine 10 mg twice daily, risperidone 0.5 mg twice daily (for agitation), trazodone 50 mg at bedtime (for sleep). Fall history: two falls in the past three months — the current bedside fall and a fall in the dining area at the prior facility while ambulating without her walker. Gait: unsteady with walker; the resident frequently does not use the walker and attempts to stand and ambulate unassisted, particularly at night. ADLs: requires verbal prompting and hands-on assistance for grooming, dressing, and bathing; continent of bowel and bladder with scheduled toileting every 2-3 hours. Behaviors: constant intermittent pacing within the unit (non-exit-seeking — does not approach exterior doors); moderate resistance to care (verbal protest during bathing, occasional pulling away during medication administration); no aggression. No diabetes, no skin breakdown. Currently receiving weekly physical therapy at the prior memory care setting. Family is requesting AFH placement closer to home to support more frequent visits. The prior memory care facility has expressed willingness to continue care but cannot consistently prevent overnight falls without 1:1 staffing that they are not contracted to provide.
inputs.family_notes: My grandmother has had Alzheimer's for over a decade and is now quite advanced — she doesn't always recognize me anymore though she still smiles when I sing to her. She has fallen twice recently, once hitting her head at night. She paces a lot and we worry about more falls. She is on medications to help with agitation but she still has hard moments. She is not aggressive, mostly anxious and restless. She does not have diabetes. She uses a walker sometimes but doesn't remember to use it consistently — that's a big concern. She has had a physical therapist working with her weekly which has helped a little. We want a home where she can be watched closely, especially at night. She is gentle and loves to be sung to and to hold soft blankets or stuffed animals.

expected_care_plan_elements: Overnight close-supervision plan: bed alarm, motion-sensor lighting, low-bed positioning
Bedside commode and scheduled night-time toileting offers (before resident attempts unassisted)
Risperidone effectiveness and continued-need review with prescriber (FRID + antipsychotic in advanced dementia)
Trazodone reassessment for sleep — consider non-pharmacologic alternatives
Continuation of weekly PT — confirm provider can travel to new AFH or arrange transfer of care
Comfort interventions integrated into routine (singing, soft blankets, stuffed animals)
expected_capability_gaps: Cognitive-mobility mismatch: resident has a walker but is fully disoriented and forgets to use it — this is one of the highest-risk fall profiles. AFH must commit to constant line-of-sight supervision during ambulation OR transition to wheelchair-only mobility plan with the family's informed consent; partial measures will not prevent recurrence

Prior facility could not safely prevent overnight falls without 1:1 — the smaller AFH staff-to-resident ratio may help, but the AFH must explicitly disclose what overnight supervision intensity it can provide and whether it considers this resident accepted
Risperidone in advanced dementia carries elevated mortality warning (FDA black box) — care plan should document the family's informed-consent discussion

care_plan.summary: Resident E is an 84-year-old female with confirmed advanced-stage Alzheimer's-type dementia, significant fall risk with two recent falls (one resulting in a head laceration requiring sutures), and high ADL support needs across dressing, grooming, and bathing. She is fully disoriented, requires scheduled toileting, and has documented sundowning and agitation behaviors. Her medication regimen includes multiple fall-risk-increasing drugs (FRIDs). No diabetes is present; that care section is not applicable. The primary care priorities at intake are: nighttime fall prevention, structured dementia-informed care approaches, safe medication administration despite resistance, and seamless PT continuity. The resident's granddaughter is the primary care-plan contact and prefers in-person communication. No source disagreements are present in this profile.

dementia_care.recommendation: Implement a consistent, person-centered daily routine with familiar sensory anchors (e.g., soft music/singing, tactile comfort objects such as soft blankets or stuffed animals) to reduce agitation and support engagement given advanced-stage Alzheimer's.
dementia_care.rationale: Resident is fully disoriented with a decade-long Alzheimer's course and an MMSE of 4/30. Family notes she responds positively to singing and soft tactile objects even when she does not recognize family members. Consistent routine and sensory-based engagement are appropriate for advanced Alzheimer's with marked cognitive loss.

dementia_care.recommendation: Use a calm, non-confrontational approach and allow additional time when providing personal care, particularly bathing and medication administration, to reduce resistance episodes. Avoid forced or rushed interactions.
dementia_care.rationale: Resident exhibits verbal protest during bathing and occasional physical pulling away during medication administration. Family confirms she is not aggressive but can have 'hard moments' of anxiety and restlessness. A de-escalation and patience-first approach aligns with her documented resistance pattern.

dementia_care.recommendation: Provide enhanced nighttime supervision and structured evening calming routines to address sundowning-pattern behaviors (increased restlessness and unassisted ambulation attempts at night).
dementia_care.rationale: The discharge summary documents nighttime unassisted ambulation attempts as a recurrent pattern, and the operator confirmed this is consistent with sundowning. The family specifically requests close nighttime supervision. The prior facility explicitly could not safely manage her overnight without 1:1 staffing.

dementia_care.recommendation: Ensure care-plan communication and updates are delivered to the resident's granddaughter in person, as she is the designated primary contact and decision-maker. Schedule regular in-person care-plan meetings to support family engagement.
dementia_care.rationale: The operator identified the granddaughter as the sole care-plan contact and decision-maker. Communication preference is documented as in-person only. A formal responsible-party name is not yet on file and should be confirmed at intake.

dementia_care.recommendation: Do not rely on verbal instruction or written reminders for safety compliance (e.g., walker use); all safety strategies must be environmental or staff-supervised, given the resident's full disorientation and inability to retain new information.
dementia_care.rationale: MMSE of 4/30 and full disorientation to person, place, and time mean the resident cannot be expected to respond to verbal cueing for safety behaviors independently. Walker non-use is documented as a consistent pattern driven by cognitive inability to remember, not behavioral refusal.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Implement a low bed with a bed alarm as the primary nighttime fall-prevention intervention, given the documented unwitnessed bedside fall at night resulting in a head laceration.
fall_risk_care.rationale: The most recent fall was a nighttime bedside fall resulting in a forehead laceration requiring sutures. The operator identified a low bed with bed alarm as a minimum required modification. The prior facility's inability to prevent overnight falls without 1:1 staffing underscores the urgency.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Install grab bars in the bathroom and ensure all pathways — especially the resident's bedroom-to-bathroom route — are clear and unobstructed at all times to support safe ambulation during nighttime toileting.
fall_risk_care.rationale: Resident paces constantly and attempts nighttime unassisted ambulation. Operator identified grab bars and clear pathways as required environmental modifications. Scheduled toileting every 2–3 hours increases nighttime pathway traffic.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Ensure the walker is physically placed within immediate reach at all times (bedside at night, beside chair during the day) and that staff provide hands-on walker prompting — not verbal-only reminders — with every ambulation attempt.
fall_risk_care.rationale: Resident has documented unsteady gait and frequently forgets to use her walker, which was directly implicated in the dining-area fall. Family and operator both confirm inconsistent walker use as a primary concern. Given full disorientation, physical placement and staff-assisted prompting are necessary.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Request a PT handoff plan from the prior memory-care facility's physical therapist before or at move-in, and arrange for continuation of weekly physical therapy services in the AFH setting.
fall_risk_care.rationale: Resident is currently receiving weekly PT at her prior facility with documented benefit to mobility. No transfer protocol or provider name has been obtained. The operator has identified this as an open action item requiring direct contact with the prior PT.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Request a prescriber review of the current medication regimen with specific attention to fall-risk-increasing drugs (FRIDs): risperidone, trazodone, donepezil, and memantine — particularly in the context of nighttime falls and sedation risk.
fall_risk_care.rationale: All four current medications are classified as FRIDs by the operator. Risperidone and trazodone carry particular concern given nighttime sedation and the documented pattern of nighttime unassisted ambulation. Operator flagged this for prescriber review.

fall_risk_care.recommendation: Conduct a room-level environmental safety assessment prior to move-in and implement all identified modifications (low bed, bed alarm, grab bars, clear pathways, and perimeter/door alarm if indicated) as a coordinated package — not piecemeal.
fall_risk_care.rationale: The operator identified a minimum of four environmental modifications required. The prior facility's discharge note confirms that environmental measures alone were insufficient without adequate overnight staffing, emphasizing that modifications must be comprehensive and concurrent.

adl_support.recommendation: Provide hands-on assistance and verbal prompting for all grooming, dressing, and bathing tasks. Approach bathing with a calm, unhurried manner and be prepared for verbal protest and occasional physical resistance; do not proceed forcefully.
adl_support.rationale: Resident requires extensive assistance for grooming, dressing, and bathing per the discharge summary. Resistance during bathing (verbal protest) and medication administration (occasional pulling away) is documented. Family confirms she is gentle but has hard moments.

adl_support.recommendation: Maintain a scheduled toileting program every 2–3 hours throughout the day and night to preserve continence, and document any changes from the established schedule that result in incontinence episodes.
adl_support.rationale: Resident is continent of bowel and bladder specifically because of scheduled toileting every 2–3 hours. This is an active, maintained intervention — not passive continence — and must be carried forward in the AFH setting to prevent regression.

adl_support.recommendation: Incorporate preferred comfort objects (soft blankets, stuffed animals) and music/singing into ADL routines to ease transitions and reduce resistance during personal care.
adl_support.rationale: Family notes the resident responds positively to singing and soft tactile objects even when she does not recognize family. These sensory strategies can serve as de-escalation tools during care resistance episodes documented in bathing and medication administration.

medication_management.recommendation: Administer all medications using a calm, patient approach; anticipate occasional physical resistance (pulling away) during medication administration and do not use restraint or force. Consider alternative administration timing or format in consultation with the prescriber if resistance is persistent.
medication_management.rationale: The discharge summary documents occasional pulling away during medication administration as a recurring resistance behavior. This is not aggressive resistance but requires a structured, de-escalation-first approach to ensure safe and complete medication delivery.

medication_management.recommendation: Flag risperidone and trazodone for prescriber review at the earliest opportunity, noting the resident's documented nighttime fall history and FRID burden. Any dosage or timing changes should be made only on prescriber order and communicated to the granddaughter.
medication_management.rationale: Operator identified risperidone (antipsychotic) and trazodone (sedating sleep aid) as the most concerning FRIDs given nighttime unassisted ambulation and fall history. Prescriber review is recommended prior to or shortly after move-in. Family granddaughter is the decision-maker for care-plan changes.

medication_management.recommendation: Document medication administration outcomes daily, including any refusals or resistance episodes, and share patterns with the prescriber and granddaughter at care-plan reviews.
medication_management.rationale: Agitation is partially controlled but not fully managed on the current regimen (family confirms ongoing 'hard moments'). Systematic documentation of administration challenges and behavioral patterns will support informed prescriber and family decision-making.

medication_management.recommendation: Maintain the full current medication list (donepezil 10 mg daily, memantine 10 mg twice daily, risperidone 0.5 mg twice daily, trazodone 50 mg at bedtime) without modification at move-in; all changes require prescriber authorization.
medication_management.rationale: The discharge summary specifies all four medications with doses and indications. No prescriber has authorized changes. Continuity of the current regimen at intake is the safe default while FRID review is coordinated.

open_questions_for_followup: PT handoff: Obtain the name and contact information of the physical therapist at the prior memory-care facility and secure a written transfer/handoff plan (including transfer training protocol and any home-exercise program) before or at move-in.

Responsible party documentation: A formal responsible-party name is not yet on file. Confirm the granddaughter's legal status (e.g., durable power of attorney for healthcare) and collect formal documentation at intake.

Family communication preference: Confirm the granddaughter's preferred communication method and frequency beyond in-person visits (e.g., phone for urgent matters, scheduled care-plan meeting cadence).

Prescriber FRID review: Schedule a medication review with the prescribing provider to assess the appropriateness of risperidone and trazodone in light of documented nighttime fall risk, with a goal of completing this review within the first 30 days of placement.

Nighttime staffing adequacy: Confirm that the AFH's overnight staffing model can provide the level of supervision required to safely manage this resident's nighttime ambulation attempts, given that her prior facility explicitly could not without 1:1 staffing.

Environmental safety assessment: Complete the room-level fall-prevention modification checklist (low bed, bed alarm, grab bars, pathway clearance, door/perimeter alarm) prior to move-in day and document completion.

Hypertension and chronic insomnia management: The discharge summary notes hypertension and chronic insomnia as part of the past medical history, but no specific medications or monitoring protocols for these conditions are documented. Clarify whether any additional management (e.g., antihypertensive medications, BP monitoring schedule) is in place.
